note: Qualitative capability matrix + exit codes, not an n=30 distribution. Elixir's compiler type output is deterministic for a pinned version (1.20.2 here), so these reproduce verbatim rather than drift. Every 'compiler_output' string is the type message recorded firsthand. No @spec and no type annotations were used anywhere, so each warning is produced by inference alone.
subject: Elixir 1.20.2 set-theoretic (gradual) type checker — see manifest.json for full environment.

support_matrix.mechanism: The if/else makes value_or_error an integer-or-binary; Map.fetch!/2 wants a map, so the two are disjoint and the checker flags it — no annotation needed.
User.name/1 does Map.fetch!(map, :name); CallsUser passes %{}, so the required :name key is provably absent.
data.a and data.b narrow data to a map with numeric fields, then adds them — internally consistent, so the checker stays quiet. This is a false-positive it correctly avoids.

support_matrix.compiler_output: `data` narrowed to `%{..., a: float() or integer()}`, then used as a number
support_matrix.mechanism: data.a forces data to be a map with a numeric :a field; the same expression then adds the whole map (data) as if it were a number — a contradiction the checker catches.

support_matrix.compiler_output: 2 warnings: an 'always matches' on is_integer(x) and a 'never matches' on is_binary(x)
support_matrix.mechanism: classify/1 has an is_integer(x) guard, so inside the cond is_integer(x) is always true and is_binary(x) can never be true — the checker flags both the redundant and the unreachable clause.

passthrough/1 does Map.fetch!(x, :any_key) on an unconstrained x. With no guard or prior field access there is no narrowing information, so the value stays fully dynamic() and escapes the checker. This is the honest limit — see caveats.

exit_code_semantics.claim: Type findings are WARNINGS, not errors, by default — only `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` turns them into a build failure. This is the honest nuance: the checker catching a bug does not, on its own, stop a build or a deploy.

cases.note: --warnings-as-errors is a *mix* concept; elixirc ignores it. Even a plain unused-variable warning does not flip elixirc's exit code.
The type warning is printed, but a plain compile does not fail the build.
The one incantation of the three that makes type warnings bite — this is what a CI job must run for the checker to gate a merge.

caveats.note: By default these findings are warnings, not errors. They do not fail a build unless you opt in via `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` (see exit_code_semantics). The checker informs; it does not enforce unless configured to.

No inference signal means no warning. The fully-dynamic gradual_escape case stays quiet not because it is correct but because the checker had nothing to narrow. Absence of a warning is NOT proof of correctness.

No @spec and no type annotations were used in any fixture. Every warning above came from inference alone. Adding @spec would give the checker more to work with; that stronger-signal path was not exercised here.

explicitly_not_verified.note: Compile-time cost of the type checker on a large, real codebase was NOT measured. These are tiny single-module fixtures; they say nothing about how much the analysis adds to a full-project `mix compile`.

Only Elixir 1.20.2 was exercised. The checker has been expanding release over release, so earlier versions would produce a different (generally smaller) set of these warnings. No version sweep was run.
